plumbing establishment in the city. ex-
cepting that of Maxwell g. Holling-
berry.


Not until the union had been on
strike for over week did the matter
come up before the trades council, and
it was a month before their indorse-
ment was secured. Then followed what
resulted in quite serious falling out
among the unions then having delegates
in the council. Fully one-half of the
unions withdrew from the central body.
and the building laborers. as central
body. was altogether disrupted.


Probably the cost to the union
plumbers of this short strike will ag-
gregate over $4,000 in wages, savings
spent. and other incidentals. The re-
suits to the trades council seems well
nigh mreparable.


BY the action of the unions at last
Tuesdays meeting. when a majority
vote was taken at the trades council,
wages for the year of 1904 will remain
as they now stand. and this decision Is
said to have influenced the plumbers
to consider returning to work. -.
